source.title: Early childhood deprivation is associated with alterations in adult brain structure despite subsequent environmental enrichment.

confidence_rationale: Provides the structural-brain substrate for INV-043's claim. Adult Romanian adoptees (n=67) show 8.6% reduction in total brain volume vs nondeprived UK adoptees (n=21), with TBV negatively correlated with deprivation duration. The structural changes statistically mediate the relationship between deprivation and adult ADHD/IQ outcomes. This is the structural counterpart to the clinical-trajectory paper (Sonuga-Barke 2017); together they make the case that the developmental window leaves architectural trace that subsequent enrichment does not erase.
failure_signatures: If INV-043 were wrong and the architecture is fully malleable post-window, brain structure should normalise after extended adoptive-family enrichment. It does not -- a quantifiable structural deficit persists into adulthood.
If the brain-structure changes were unrelated to functional outcomes, mediation tests should fail. They held -- TBV reduction statistically mediated both lower IQ and higher ADHD symptoms.

mapping.source_claim_statement: Romanian adoptees (n=67, with 3-41 months of deprivation) showed 8.6% reduced total brain volume in young adulthood compared with non-deprived UK adoptees (n=21), with TBV negatively correlated with deprivation duration. Right inferior frontal surface area and volume were lower; right inferior temporal lobe was greater (interpreted as compensatory). Right medial prefrontal volume scaled positively with deprivation duration. TBV reductions mediated the deprivation-to-IQ and deprivation-to-ADHD-symptoms paths.
mapping.ree_translation: INV-043 needs a substrate-level account of how the early caregiving window shapes architecture. Mackes provides it: adult brain structure shows persistent deprivation-correlated changes despite intervening enrichment. The architecture is not 'reset' by post-adoption caregiving in the way that pure architectural-capacity readings would predict. The medial PFC and inferior frontal regions implicated are, broadly, the regions REE associates with executive control, social cognition, and (in the V3/V4 transition) committed planning -- all relevant to ethical agency.
mapping.mapping_caveat: n=21 in the control group is small; the structural-MRI-from-childhood-deprivation literature has had replication challenges. The compensatory-temporal-lobe finding is speculative. Brain structure does not directly demonstrate ethical-functioning consequences -- the clinical and behavioural outcomes are documented in the Sonuga-Barke 2017 trajectory paper, not here. The deprivation is multi-dimensional, not caregiver-specific.
mapping.source_context: Subset of the ERA cohort with structural MRI in young adulthood; 67 Romanian adoptees vs 21 UK adoptees.

confidence_components.notes: PNAS, established cohort, careful covariate control. Modest control n; mapping fidelity drops because brain-structure does not directly speak to ethical outcomes.
